answer:
Short answer: **the research generally does _not_ say that interesting work barely matters**. If anything, it says the opposite:

- **Interesting/intrinsically motivating work matters a lot for job satisfaction**
- It matters **somewhat less for overall life happiness**
- And it is **not the only thing that matters**—autonomy, manager quality, hours, pressure, coworkers, and fit often matter just as much or more

So if your question is:

> “Is there evidence that people aren’t much happier when they choose the most interesting job possible?”

I’d say: **not really as a general claim**. The better-supported claim is:

> **Interesting work improves well-being, but not enough to dominate all other job features.**

## What the literature tends to show

### 1. Intrinsic job characteristics are strong predictors of job satisfaction
A large body of organizational psychology shows that people are happier with jobs that have things like:

- autonomy
- skill variety
- challenge
- task significance / meaning
- feedback
- opportunities to learn

This goes back to the classic **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses of work design. “Interesting work” is often not measured as a single item; instead it’s captured through these dimensions.

The broad result is very consistent: **jobs that feel engaging, meaningful, and growth-producing are associated with substantially higher job satisfaction and engagement**.

### 2. Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once income is already high
There’s also a lot of evidence that **pay and happiness are related, but only modestly**, especially once someone is already well-compensated.

For someone deciding between two already-excellent jobs at Google and Meta, the extra salary often has **less impact on day-to-day experience** than people imagine.

That doesn’t mean compensation is irrelevant. It matters for:

- security
- optionality
- family planning
- savings / FIRE goals
- stress reduction

But if the salary difference is “a bit higher” rather than life-changing, research would generally predict that **the quality of the work and environment could matter more**.

### 3. Meaningful or interesting work affects job happiness more than life happiness
This distinction matters a lot.

Research tends to find a stronger effect on:

- **job satisfaction**
- **engagement**
- **burnout risk**
- **positive affect at work**

than on:

- global life satisfaction
- overall happiness across all domains of life

That makes sense: work is important, but it’s only one part of life. A more interesting job won’t fully compensate for:

- bad sleep
- long commute
- poor health
- weak relationships
- toxic manager
- constant pressure

So if someone says “interesting work is everything,” the literature does **not** support that extreme position.

### 4. People adapt, and they also mispredict
There is good behavioral research showing people often make forecasting errors:

- they **overestimate** how much a raise will change their long-term happiness
- they also often **overestimate** how much one job feature alone will determine happiness

This is related to **hedonic adaptation** and **focalism**. When imagining a job, we focus on the salient thing—“cool research topic” or “finally leading a team”—and underweight the daily texture of the role.

So a more precise summary is:

- **interesting work matters**
- but people often **overestimate the lasting impact of any single attribute**

## Important nuance in your specific case

Your framing is:

- Google: **very interesting work**
- Meta: **less interesting work**, but
  - a bit more pay
  - leading a small team
  - something you’ve always wanted

That means this is **not really** a simple “interesting vs money” tradeoff.

It’s more like:

- **interesting technical content**
vs
- **leadership growth + status + a different kind of intrinsic reward**

And that’s important, because leading a team may itself be deeply meaningful and motivating for you.

A lot depends on **what kind of interest** matters most to you:

### Type A: content interest
“I love the topic, the research problem, the intellectual puzzle.”

### Type B: role interest
“I love mentoring, setting direction, building a team, multiplying impact.”

Many people confuse these. They say “this job is less interesting,” but what they really mean is “the domain is less exciting.” Yet the role itself may be more aligned with who they want to become.

## What research would predict for a person like you

If I had to make an evidence-based guess:

- If you are someone who gets most of your energy from **deep technical/research novelty**, then moving to a less interesting area may reduce day-to-day satisfaction more than the salary bump will compensate.
- If you have a strong unmet desire to **lead**, mentor, and shape work through others, then the Meta role may actually produce **higher well-being**, even if the subject matter is less exciting.

In other words:

> **The question is not “Is the work less interesting?”**
>  
> It is:
> **“Will my average week feel more alive in this role?”**

## One thing the research definitely does *not* say
It does **not** say:

> “Maximize interestingness at all costs.”

That would be too simplistic.

People are often less happy in “super interesting” jobs when those jobs also come with:

- chaos
- overwork
- unstable priorities
- low control
- bad management
- constant evaluation pressure

So if Meta is less interesting but gives you:

- more autonomy
- real leadership scope
- a stronger manager
- better team fit
- more future optionality

then it could still be the better move.

Conversely, if it gives you:

- more politics
- people management without real authority
- higher pressure
- a dull product area
- less technical depth than you want

then the leadership title may wear off quickly.

## A practical way to think about this

Instead of asking which job is “better,” score the **daily drivers of well-being**.

For each role, rate 1–10:

- interest in the actual day-to-day work
- autonomy
- learning/growth
- manager quality
- teammates
- workload sustainability
- mission fit
- leadership opportunity
- future option value
- compensation

Then **weight** them by importance to you.

For example, if leadership is something you’ve “always wanted,” don’t weight it at 5%. Be honest and maybe weight it heavily.

## My bottom line
If you’re looking for empirical reassurance that “interesting work may not matter much,” I don’t think the literature really gives you that.

The stronger research-based takeaway is:

- **Interesting work usually matters a lot**
- **But not enough to dominate everything else**
- **And “interesting” includes the role itself, not just the topic**

So in your case, I would not reduce this to:

- Google = happiness
- Meta = money

I’d reduce it to:

- **Do you want to keep optimizing for fascinating problems?**
- or **is it time to optimize for becoming the kind of leader you’ve wanted to be?**

That second thing can be just as intrinsically rewarding as the first.

If you want, I can also give you:
1. a **research-paper-oriented answer with citations**, or  
2. a **decision framework specifically for your Google vs Meta situation**.

answer:
Short answer: the research does **not** generally say “interesting work barely matters.” If anything, it usually says the opposite:

- **Interesting, meaningful, intrinsically motivating work is strongly associated with job satisfaction**
- But it is **less strongly tied to overall life happiness** than people often assume
- And people often **overweight one salient factor** (“interesting research” or “higher salary”) and underweight what daily life in the job will actually feel like

So your intuition is partly right, but not in the exact way you phrased it.

## What the research usually finds

### 1. “Interesting work” matters a lot for **job satisfaction**
Several big traditions in organizational psychology point in the same direction:

- **Job Characteristics Model**: work tends to be more satisfying when it has
  - variety
  - autonomy
  - significance
  - feedback
  - a sense of ownership

- **Self-Determination Theory**: people do better when work supports
  - **autonomy**
  - **competence**
  - **relatedness**

- Research on **meaningful work** also tends to show positive links with
  - engagement
  - satisfaction
  - persistence
  - lower burnout risk

So if by “interesting” you mean something like:
- intellectually engaging
- high learning
- challenging in a good way
- aligned with your values
- lets you do work you care about

then yes, research generally says that matters a lot.

## 2. But “interesting” is not the same as “happier overall”
This is where your skepticism has more support.

Work is only one part of life, and overall happiness is also heavily influenced by things like:
- stress level
- hours
- sleep
- manager quality
- coworkers
- commute
- family impact
- sense of control
- job security
- culture/politics

A job can be very interesting but still make you less happy if it is:
- chaotic
- high-conflict
- relentlessly urgent
- politically draining
- bad for family/life balance

So the literature is more like:

> interesting work is usually good for satisfaction, but it does not automatically dominate all other quality-of-life factors.

## 3. People are bad at forecasting this
There’s a well-known idea in behavioral research—often associated with Kahneman and others—that people show a **focusing illusion**:

> when deciding, we exaggerate the importance of the thing we’re currently thinking about.

In your case, that could be either:
- “the work is so interesting at Google”
or
- “I’ve always wanted to lead a team at Meta”

Both may be real. But when imagining the future, people often underweight:
- what their Tuesday afternoon will feel like
- how much context-switching they’ll do
- whether they’ll feel drained after work
- what kinds of frustrations recur every week

That’s often more predictive of well-being than the headline label of the role.

## 4. Salary usually has diminishing returns
Research here is also pretty consistent:

- more money helps a lot when it reduces real stress or fixes a sense of unfairness
- beyond that, the effect on day-to-day well-being gets smaller
- pay still matters for status, optionality, and feeling valued—but often less than people predict

So “Meta pays a bit more” is definitely not irrelevant. But if it’s only **a bit** more, and both options already put you in a very comfortable range, then salary alone is probably not the deciding happiness variable.

## 5. Leadership can increase satisfaction — but only if you like the actual work of management
This is probably the key point in your situation.

A lot of people say they want to “lead a team,” but what they actually want is one of these:
- more influence
- more ownership
- more recognition
- more mentoring
- more strategic input

That is not always the same thing as enjoying management.

The day-to-day of leading a small team usually includes:
- hiring
- performance reviews
- conflict management
- alignment meetings
- stakeholder management
- career conversations
- protecting the team
- saying “no”
- less time on the fun technical/research problems

If that sounds energizing, the Meta role may be more intrinsically rewarding than it first appears, even if the technical content is less interesting.

If what you really love is deep research work, then the “leadership dream” can turn out to be a prestige fantasy rather than a satisfying reality.

## So: is there evidence that maximizing “interestingness” doesn’t help much?
I’d phrase it this way:

- I’m **not aware of a strong research consensus** saying “people are not much happier when they choose the most interesting job possible.”
- The broader evidence says:
  - **intrinsically interesting/meaningful work usually helps**
  - but **not enough to compensate for bad daily conditions**
  - and people often **overestimate** how much one salient feature will determine their future happiness

So the smart takeaway is **not** “interesting work doesn’t matter.”
It’s:

> interesting work matters, but it is only one part of the lived experience of the job.

## For your specific decision
I’d suggest evaluating the two roles on these dimensions, not just “interesting vs money”:

### Google
- How sustainable is the work?
- How much autonomy do you have?
- Can you get leadership opportunities internally in the next 1–2 years?
- Are you still growing, or just comfortable?
- Does the work still excite you weekly, or just in principle?

### Meta
- Would you truly enjoy managing people?
- How much of the role is people leadership vs coordination/politics?
- What is the pressure level and culture of the org?
- Is the work “less interesting” but still good enough?
- Will this role open doors you deeply want later?

## A useful decision test
Try this:

### Ask “which regret is worse in 2 years?”
1. “I stayed at Google, kept doing fascinating work, but postponed leadership again.”
2. “I took Meta, became a manager, but I miss doing genuinely exciting research every day.”

Whichever regret feels sharper is informative.

## My read, based on what you wrote
The most important unresolved question is not salary. It’s this:

> Do you want leadership itself, or do you want the identity/status of being someone who leads?

If it’s the first, the Meta role could make sense despite less interesting content.

If it’s the second, you may be giving up something genuinely valuable—interesting work—for a fantasy version of management.

If you want, I can also help you think through this as a concrete decision framework, with a weighted scorecard for Google vs Meta.

But it also does **not** say you should maximize “interestingness” at any cost, because other factors—especially **autonomy, manager quality, team relationships, workload, and fit with your longer-term goals**—often matter just as much or more.

So if you’re hoping to find research saying “don’t worry, interestingness barely affects happiness,” that’s probably **not** the best reading of the evidence.

## What the research tends to show

A few well-established lines of research are relevant:

### 1. Intrinsic motivation and meaningful work matter a lot
Across organizational psychology, people are usually happier and more engaged when work is:
- interesting
- meaningful
- challenging in a good way
- aligned with their strengths
- giving them a sense of progress

Classic frameworks like the **Job Characteristics Model** and **Self-Determination Theory** point in the same direction:
- **Skill variety / task significance / autonomy / feedback** predict satisfaction and motivation
- **Autonomy, competence, and relatedness** predict well-being

This doesn’t mean “interesting work is everything,” but it does mean it’s a real contributor, not a myth.

### 2. “Interesting” is often a proxy for better things underneath
When people say a job is interesting, they often also mean:
- “I’m learning”
- “I care about the problem”
- “I feel energized”
- “I can use my strengths”
- “I’m proud of what I’m building”

Those deeper features are strongly associated with higher job satisfaction.

### 3. But the effect is not infinite
The phrase **“most interesting job possible”** is too strong.

Research does **not** imply that you should optimize only for technical interest, because:
- people adapt to exciting work
- highly interesting jobs can also be stressful, political, unstable, or all-consuming
- boring work can be tolerable if it comes with great autonomy, great colleagues, low stress, and strong compensation
- “interesting now” may conflict with “who I want to become in 5 years”

So the evidence is more like:
> Interesting work tends to improve well-being, but only as one part of a broader system.

### 4. Salary helps, but with diminishing returns
Compensation matters, especially for reducing stress and increasing freedom. But once you’re already highly compensated, **incremental salary gains usually have smaller effects on happiness** than people expect.

For someone choosing between Google and Meta in senior technical roles, the extra money may matter less to day-to-day well-being than:
- who you work with
- how much control you have
- whether you’re growing
- whether the work feels worth doing

### 5. Leadership can increase happiness for some people
This is especially relevant in your case.

If **leading a team is something you’ve “always wanted to do,”** then the Meta offer may be more interesting than it first appears—just not in the technical-content dimension.

There are different kinds of interest:
- interest in the **problem**
- interest in the **role**
- interest in the **trajectory**
- interest in the **people side**
- interest in **having more leverage**

A less interesting research topic might still be a more interesting **life experiment** if it lets you test whether you actually enjoy management.

That matters because career regret often comes less from “I worked on a less exciting project” and more from:
- “I never tried the path I was curious about”
- “I stayed because it was comfortable”
- “I optimized for the wrong dimension”

## So is there research showing people are *not much happier* when they choose the most interesting job?

I’d put it like this:

- **No strong general conclusion** says interesting work barely matters.
- The literature more often finds that **intrinsic interest / meaning / fit do predict higher job satisfaction**.
- However, there is also good evidence that people **mispredict** what will make them happy, and they often **overweight prestige, salary, or one salient feature** while underweighting daily lived experience.

In other words:
- You probably **shouldn’t dismiss interesting work**
- But you also probably **shouldn’t treat “technical interestingness” as the sole criterion**

## The biggest practical question in your situation

Your choice doesn’t sound like:

- interesting work vs uninteresting work

It sounds more like:

- **interesting technical work at Google**
vs
- **interesting leadership growth at Meta**

That is a much harder and more consequential trade-off.

## A useful way to think about it

Ask yourself which regret feels more likely in 2–3 years:

### If you stay at Google
Will you think:
- “I’m glad I kept doing truly interesting work”
or
- “I had a chance to start leading and I let it pass”

### If you go to Meta
Will you think:
- “I’m glad I tested management and expanded my scope”
or
- “I traded away the part of work that actually energizes me”

## What tends to matter most in outcomes like this

In practice, people are often happiest when they optimize for a combination of:

1. **Daily enjoyment**

2. **Growth**

3. **Autonomy**  
   How much control do I have over priorities and style?

4. **People**

5. **Sustainability**  
   Is this life actually livable?

6. **Optionality**

Meta leadership might score high on growth and optionality.  
Google research might score high on daily enjoyment and intrinsic motivation.

## One thing research strongly supports: don’t ignore the manager/team
This may matter more than you think.

A somewhat less interesting job with:
- a great manager
- clear support
- healthy culture
- real authority
- strong peers

can be better than a highly interesting job with:
- chaos

- constant reorgs
- impossible expectations

So if you evaluate the Meta offer, look very hard at:
- who you’d report to
- how much actual authority you’d have
- whether “leading a small team” is real or nominal

- whether the team is set up to win

## My honest synthesis

If your only question is:

> “Does research suggest interesting work doesn’t matter much for happiness?”

- interest in day-to-day work
- autonomy
- learning

- team quality
- stress / sustainability
- compensation
- future optionality

Then weight them honestly.

A lot of people discover that “interesting work” is maybe 25–35% of the decision, not 80%.

### 1. Intrinsic motivation matters
People are generally happier and more engaged when work feels:
- interesting
- meaningful
- challenging in a good way
- aligned with their strengths

This is consistent with:
- **Self-Determination Theory**: people do better when work supports **autonomy, competence, and relatedness**
- **Job Characteristics Theory**: work is more motivating when it has skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback

So yes: **interesting work is real, and it matters**.

### 2. But “interestingness” has diminishing returns
Going from boring to reasonably engaging tends to help a lot.

Going from “already pretty interesting” to “maximally fascinating” often helps less than people imagine, especially if the new role is worse on things like:
- stress
- politics
- control
- team quality
- manager quality
- hours
- uncertainty
- mismatch with long-term goals

In other words: **once work is above some threshold of interest, happiness differences are often driven by other features**.

### 3. People often overpredict how much one feature will matter
There’s a large literature on **affective forecasting** showing that people are not great at predicting what will make them happy long term.

People often overweight:
- salary
- prestige
- the excitement of the work itself

and underweight:
- daily experience
- manager and coworkers
- whether they feel effective
- whether they have energy after work
- whether the role fits their identity

So “this project is intellectually amazing” may matter less than expected if the day-to-day is frustrating.

### 4. Meaningful/interesting work can even come with tradeoffs
There’s also research and plenty of real-world evidence that people in highly meaningful or prestigious work sometimes tolerate:
- worse boundaries
- overwork
- identity enmeshment
- disappointment
- lower pay or less control

So “interesting” is not a free good. It can come bundled with pressure or obsession.

## Is there evidence that people are *not much happier* when they choose the most interesting job?

I’d phrase it like this:

- There is **good evidence that more interesting work is associated with higher job satisfaction**
- But I’m not aware of strong evidence for the simpler claim:  
  **“maximize interest above all else and you’ll be much happier”**
- In fact, a lot of research points the other way: **well-being depends on a bundle of job characteristics, not just interest**

If two jobs are both decent, the happiness gap may be quite small unless one is clearly worse on fundamentals.

That seems close to what you’re asking.

## For your specific case

Your choice is not:

- interesting work vs uninteresting work

It’s more like:

- **Google**: more interesting work, current IC path
- **Meta**: somewhat less interesting work, but higher salary and first people-management opportunity

That changes the analysis, because “leading a small team” is not just a perk — it may itself be deeply satisfying if what you want is:
- more scope
- mentorship
- influence
- ownership
- identity growth

For some people, that is **more meaningful** than the technical topic itself.

So the real comparison may be:

- **content interest**
vs
- **role interest / developmental interest**

Those are different kinds of “interesting.”

## A useful way to think about it

Try scoring each role 1–10 on these, separately:

1. **Day-to-day enjoyment**
2. **Long-term career capital**
3. **Autonomy**
4. **Learning/growth**
5. **People you’ll work with**
6. **Manager quality**
7. **Stress / sustainability**
8. **Meaning / pride**
9. **Compensation**
10. **Option value**  
   Which role keeps more doors open in 2–3 years?

Then add two extra questions:

### A. Which regret feels heavier?
- “I stayed too long doing fascinating work and delayed learning leadership”
- “I left fascinating work too early for management and missed what I really love”

### B. What kind of pain are you choosing?
Every good career option has a downside:
- Google downside: maybe slower path into leadership, maybe frustration about not trying management
- Meta downside: less interesting content, possible risk that you miss deep research work

Often the best choice is the downside you can live with.

## My read, based on what you wrote

The phrase that stands out is:

> “leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do”

That sounds important. Not a random bonus — a genuine aspiration.

If that’s true, then the Meta role may not actually be “less interesting” in the sense that matters for your life. The technical domain may be less interesting, but the **human and leadership challenge** may be more interesting.

That said, I would be cautious if:
- you mainly love doing deep research yourself
- the Meta role is vague or politically risky
- the team quality or manager quality is unclear
- the salary bump is not actually meaningful to your life
- you suspect you’re romanticizing management

A lot of strong ICs think they want management, but what they really want is:
- more influence
- more strategic input
- mentoring
- project ownership

Sometimes you can get that without becoming a manager.

## A practical decision rule

You probably shouldn’t move **just** for a bit more money.

You probably also shouldn’t stay **just** because the work is technically more interesting.

A move seems worth it if most of these are true:
- you genuinely want to test management soon
- the team seems strong
- the manager above you is good
- the domain is “less interesting” but still acceptable, not soul-draining
- the role increases your future option value
- you’d regret not trying it

Staying seems better if most of these are true:
- the Google work is unusually exciting and hard to replicate
- the Meta role mainly attracts you because it signals progress
- you’re unsure you actually want people management
- you can likely get leadership scope internally within a reasonable time
- the day-to-day content mismatch at Meta would wear on you

## Bottom line

So to answer your research question directly:

**No, the evidence does not support a simple rule that people become much happier by choosing the most interesting job possible.**  
**Yes, interesting work usually helps — but happiness is strongly shaped by many other job features, and maximizing “interestingness” alone is often not the best predictor of well-being.**

In your case, I’d focus less on:
- “Which company has more interesting subject matter?”

and more on:
- “Which role gives me the better total package of daily satisfaction, growth, and future identity?”

If you want, I can help you do a more concrete **decision matrix** for Google vs Meta, or help think through whether you actually want **management** versus **staff-level IC influence**.

answer:
Short answer: the research does **not** say “interesting work barely matters.”  
If anything, it says the opposite: **interesting/meaningful/intrinsically motivating work is usually a fairly strong predictor of job satisfaction and somewhat of overall well-being**.

But it also says something important that is easy to miss:

> **Choosing the “most interesting possible” job is not the same as choosing the job that will make you happiest.**

Because beyond a certain point, other factors often matter just as much or more:
- autonomy
- manager quality
- workload and stress
- team relationships
- growth
- feeling effective
- fairness/politics
- flexibility

So the evidence is more like: **interesting work matters a lot, but it is only one dimension of a good work life.**

## What the literature generally finds

Researchers usually study this under labels like:
- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job characteristics**
- **person-job fit**

rather than “interestingness” exactly.

### 1) Meaningful/intrinsically motivating work is positively associated with well-being
A lot of organizational psychology points in the same direction:

- Jobs with more **variety, autonomy, significance, and feedback** tend to produce higher job satisfaction.
- People who experience their work as **meaningful** tend to report higher job satisfaction, engagement, and lower burnout.
- Better **fit** between what you enjoy/value and what the job actually is tends to improve satisfaction.

This is a pretty robust finding, not some tiny fringe effect.

### 2) But the effect is not infinite
Research also suggests that “more interesting” does not create unlimited happiness.

Two reasons:

#### A. Diminishing returns
The jump from **boring/misaligned** to **engaging** is big.  
The jump from **quite interesting** to **maximally fascinating** is often much smaller.

#### B. Other job features can swamp it
A fascinating role with:
- a bad manager,
- chaotic expectations,
- political infighting,
- unsustainable workload,

can easily make someone less happy than a somewhat less interesting role with:
- autonomy,
- supportive leadership,
- good colleagues,
- reasonable pace,
- clear scope.

This is very consistent with the broader literature.

### 3) Salary matters, but usually less than people think once you’re already well paid
For someone deciding between Google and Meta, this is especially relevant.

At high income levels, an incremental salary bump often has **much less effect on happiness** than people predict, especially compared with daily work experience.  
That doesn’t mean money is irrelevant. It means:
- money matters a lot when it changes security/options,
- but once you’re already comfortable, **the marginal happiness return is often smaller** than changes in autonomy, interest, stress, or relationships.

### 4) “Interesting” can sometimes be overrated if it becomes identity-trap work
There’s also literature around **calling** and **passion** showing a subtle downside:

People who care deeply about their work may:
- overwork,
- tolerate worse treatment,
- blur boundaries,
- attach self-worth too tightly to the role.

So “I should optimize for the most interesting thing at all costs” is **not** strongly supported.  
Meaningful work is good; **over-indexing on it** can backfire.

---

## So is there research showing people are “not much happier” in the most interesting job possible?

I’d phrase it this way:

- There is **not strong evidence** that interesting work doesn’t matter.
- There **is** good evidence that its incremental benefit is **limited and conditional**.
- The happier choice is usually not “maximize interest at all costs,” but rather “maximize the combination of interest, autonomy, fit, relationships, and sustainability.”

That’s probably the most research-consistent answer.

---

## For your specific situation

Your choice is not really:

- **interesting work** vs
- **uninteresting work**

It sounds more like:

- **more interesting individual-contributor/research content**
vs
- **less interesting domain content but a leadership role you genuinely want**

That matters, because leadership itself may satisfy major psychological needs:
- growth
- autonomy
- status
- influence
- mentoring
- building something through others

If leading a team is something you’ve “always wanted,” then the Meta role may be **less interesting topically** but **more intrinsically motivating overall**.

That is not a small distinction.

A lot of people make bad career decisions because they confuse:
- “I like the subject matter”
with
- “I will like the actual life this job creates every day”

Those are different.

---

## A useful evidence-based way to compare the jobs

Instead of asking only “Which is more interesting?”, rate each role 1–10 on:

1. **Day-to-day enjoyment**  
   Will I enjoy a normal Tuesday?

2. **Autonomy**  
   How much control will I have over what I do and how I do it?

3. **Growth**  
   Which role develops the person I want to become?

4. **Meaning/impact**  
   Will the work feel worthwhile?

5. **Relationships**  
   Manager quality, team quality, politics.

6. **Stress sustainability**  
   Can I do this for 2–3 years without becoming depleted?

7. **Identity fit**  
   Which role feels more like “me”?

8. **Option value**  
   Which role opens more doors later?

9. **Compensation**  
   Not “higher is better,” but “does this materially change my life?”

10. **Regret test**  
   In 3 years, which non-choice am I more likely to regret?

This tends to produce a much better decision than asking only about money or topic interest.

---

## My guess, based on research and on your description

If the Meta role is:
- genuinely less interesting,
- significantly more political/stressful,
- and the salary increase is only modest,

then the literature would make me cautious about moving **unless** the leadership opportunity is truly central to your long-term goals.

If, however:
- you deeply want to learn management,
- the team is strong,
- the manager is good,
- the scope is real,
- and this role gives you autonomy and development you currently lack,

then moving could absolutely make sense even if the work content is less fascinating.

Because in that case, you are not trading “interesting” for “boring.”  
You are trading one kind of intrinsic reward for another.

---

## Practical next step

Before deciding, get unusually concrete about the Meta role:

Ask:
- What does a typical week actually look like?
- How much time is management vs technical work?
- How is success measured?
- How much hiring/performance-management burden is there?
- How stable is the org?
- What is the manager like?
- Why is the role open?
- How many meetings per week?
- How much autonomy over roadmap?
- What happened to the previous lead?

And ask yourself:
- Do I want to **lead a team**, or do I want the **idea** of leading a team?
- Do I love research work, or am I just comfortable in it?
- Am I optimizing for current enjoyment, future career capital, or identity?

---

## Bottom line

The research does **not** support “interesting work doesn’t really make people happier.”  
It usually does.

But it **does** support this:

> Once a job is already reasonably good, maximizing “interestingness” alone is often not the best predictor of happiness.  
> A broader bundle — autonomy, fit, growth, manager, relationships, and sustainable stress — matters more.

If you want, I can help you think through this as a structured decision between the Google and Meta roles rather than just as a general research question.

answer:
Short answer: yes—research does **not** support the idea that you should simply maximize “interestingness” of the work and expect that to maximize happiness.

What it shows is more like:

- **Interesting/meaningful work does matter**
- but it is only **one input**
- and often **not the dominant one** once pay is “good enough”

The strongest predictors of work well-being tend to be a bundle of things: **autonomy/control, competence/growth, good colleagues/manager, manageable stress, and person-job fit**. “Interesting work” helps mainly because it often brings some of those.

## What the research says

### 1) Interesting work is positively related to job satisfaction
Organizational psychology has found for decades that people are more satisfied when work has features like:

- variety
- significance
- feedback
- autonomy

This is the classic **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses broadly support it. Work that feels engaging and significant tends to improve motivation and satisfaction.

But notice: **“interesting” is mixed together with other things**, especially autonomy and impact.

### 2) Autonomy and fit are often as important, or more important
Research from **self-determination theory** and **person-job fit** shows that well-being is strongly tied to:

- **autonomy**: feeling you have control
- **competence**: feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness**: liking/respecting the people you work with
- **fit**: the role matches what you value

So if one job is more “interesting” but another gives you more ownership, more leadership growth, and a better match to your long-term identity, the second can easily produce more satisfaction.

### 3) Pay matters, but less than people think after a certain point
Pay increases do improve satisfaction, especially if they reduce financial stress. But once compensation is already high, the incremental happiness from “a bit higher salary” is usually **modest** compared with:

- work hours
- stress
- manager quality
- control over your work
- whether you actually enjoy your day-to-day tasks

There are meta-analyses showing the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is **positive but fairly small**.

### 4) “Meaningful/interesting” work helps, but not without limit
There’s also a counterpoint in the literature and broader social science discussion: people who strongly prioritize “doing what they love” sometimes accept:

- worse boundaries
- more stress
- identity fusion with work
- disappointment when the actual job is mostly meetings, politics, and maintenance

So maximizing interest can backfire if it comes with poor conditions or if the reality of the role differs from the idealized story.

### 5) Most research is about job satisfaction, not total life happiness
This is important. A very interesting job may raise **job satisfaction** but not necessarily **overall life satisfaction** if it also brings:

- longer hours
- more pressure
- less time for family/friends
- emotional exhaustion

So the slogan “interesting work is everything” is much stronger than the evidence.

## So to your actual situation

You’re not choosing between:

- interesting job vs boring job

You’re choosing between:

- **Google:** more interesting work
- **Meta:** somewhat more money + first real team leadership opportunity + less interesting domain

That’s a much richer tradeoff.

And the research would say: the answer depends less on “which is more interesting?” and more on **what kind of satisfaction you personally are missing right now**.

### If what you most crave is intellectual stimulation
Then staying where the work is more interesting may genuinely be better for your day-to-day happiness.

Especially because leading a team often means:

- fewer hours doing the core technical work
- more hiring/performance/coaching/alignment
- more meetings
- more responsibility for others’ output

A lot of people say they want leadership, when what they really want is **more influence**, not people management.

### If what you most crave is growth, scope, and impact through others
Then the Meta role may actually be more satisfying, even if the underlying topic is less exciting.

Why? Because leadership can satisfy:

- autonomy
- competence
- status/recognition
- sense of impact
- learning

If “I always wanted to lead a small team” is a deep preference rather than a vague aspiration, that matters a lot.

## A practical way to think about it

Ask not:

- “Which role is objectively best?”
- “Which role is most interesting?”

Ask:

### 1) What makes me happiest in a normal work week?
Rank these:

- intellectual novelty
- shipping/impact
- autonomy
- prestige
- money
- learning
- mentoring/leading
- work-life balance
- good collaborators
- low stress

If “interesting research” is #1 by far, that points one way.
If “leading people and building something through a team” is near the top, that points another way.

### 2) What do I want to become in 3 years?
- deeper IC researcher?
- research/eng manager?
- technical leader with a team?

Because the Meta offer may be valuable as **career capital**, even if it’s not the most fascinating problem space right now.

### 3) What will the day-to-day actually feel like?
This is huge.

For the Meta role, try to learn:

- how much time is actual technical work vs management
- who you’d manage
- whether the team is healthy or messy
- how much autonomy you’d really have
- whether success is realistic
- what the manager above you is like

A “team lead” role under a bad manager in a less interesting space can be much worse than it sounds on paper.

### 4) Is there a version of the best of both worlds?
Before deciding, it may be worth exploring:

- can Google give you more leadership scope internally?
- can Meta shape the role to keep some technical depth?
- can you wait for a leadership opportunity in a more interesting area?

Often the best move is not choosing A vs B, but negotiating a better C.

## My bottom line

The evidence does **not** say “always choose the most interesting work possible.”
It says something more nuanced:

> Interesting work helps, but happiness at work comes more from overall fit than from interest alone.

In your case, the key question is probably:

> Do you mainly love doing interesting research yourself, or do you want to grow into leading others enough that you’d accept a less exciting domain?

If you want, I can also help you think through this with a concrete decision framework—for example a **weighted scorecard** for Google vs Meta using your own values.

answer:
Short answer: **the research does not say “interesting work barely matters.”** If anything, it says the opposite: **interest/meaning/intrinsic motivation are usually important predictors of job satisfaction and well-being**.

But it also says something more subtle:

> **People are usually not happiest by optimizing for “interestingness” alone.**  
> They’re happiest when several needs are met at once: interest, autonomy, competence, relationships, manageable stress, fair pay, and fit with the role.

So if your question is:

- **“Does research show that choosing the most interesting possible job doesn’t make people much happier?”**  
  **Not really in that simple form.**
- **“Does research show that maximizing interest alone is not the best decision rule?”**  
  **Yes, that is much closer to what the evidence suggests.**

## What the literature generally finds

A few robust themes show up repeatedly:

### 1. Interesting/meaningful work does matter
Research in:

- **job characteristics theory**
- **self-determination theory**
- **person-job fit / vocational fit**
- **meaningful work / calling research**

all points in a similar direction: people tend to be better off when their work feels:

- intrinsically engaging
- autonomous
- competence-building
- significant/meaningful
- aligned with their interests and values

So “do interesting work” is not just folk wisdom.

### 2. But its effect is not infinite
The key nuance is that **more interesting is not always much better once you’re already above some threshold**.

If Job A is fascinating but has:

- bad manager
- politics
- low autonomy
- brutal hours
- poor social environment
- no growth path

then Job B can easily produce more well-being even if the actual subject matter is less exciting.

This is very consistent with research: **daily experience at work is multi-dimensional**.

### 3. People often overweight one salient feature
There’s also a broader psychological finding that people make decisions by over-focusing on one vivid factor and underweighting others.

In your case, the vivid factor could be either:

- “the research is super interesting”
or
- “I finally get to lead a team”

In reality, your happiness 6–18 months later will probably be driven by a bundle of things, including:

- what your actual week feels like
- how much autonomy you have
- whether you respect your manager
- whether the team is good
- stress level
- whether the role fits your identity

### 4. Salary matters, but usually with diminishing returns
A “bit higher salary” helps, but unless the increase is materially changing your life, **it is often not the main long-run determinant of job happiness**.

That doesn’t mean ignore it. It just means that if the salary difference is modest relative to your current compensation, it often shouldn’t dominate the choice.

### 5. Wanting to lead may itself be a major source of meaning
This part of your description stood out:

> “leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do”

That is not a small detail. If management/leadership is a genuine aspiration, then the Meta role is not simply “less interesting.” It may be:

- less interesting in technical content,
- but more interesting in **responsibility, growth, influence, and identity**.

Those dimensions matter a lot in well-being research too.

## So what should you infer for your situation?

I would frame the choice like this:

You are probably not choosing between:

- **interesting work**
vs
- **higher salary**

You are more likely choosing between two different kinds of meaning:

### Google
- stronger intellectual interest
- likely stronger fit with your current strengths
- potentially higher day-to-day technical enjoyment

### Meta
- somewhat higher pay
- first real team leadership experience
- potentially more growth in direction you’ve wanted for a long time
- less interesting technical/problem content

That is a much harder and more legitimate tradeoff.

## What the evidence would suggest you ask yourself

### 1. What actually drives your day-to-day happiness most?
Be concrete. On a random Tuesday, what matters most?

- working on fascinating problems?
- feeling ownership?
- building things through others?
- mentoring?
- prestige?
- compensation?
- low stress?
- being around people you like?

Don’t answer aspirationally. Answer descriptively.

### 2. Do you want to be a manager, or do you want to have been a manager?
A lot of people like the idea of leadership more than the actual work of it.

Actual first-line management often means:

- hiring
- feedback
- performance management
- prioritization
- conflict handling
- less hands-on technical work
- more meetings and ambiguity

If that sounds energizing, the Meta role may be more attractive than “less interesting” suggests.

If it sounds like a tax you’ll pay in order to get status/scope, be careful.

### 3. Is the technical work at Meta “less interesting” or actively demotivating?
There’s a big difference between:

- “not my top-choice topic”
and
- “I will dread thinking about this every day”

If it’s only somewhat less interesting, leadership/growth may more than offset it.

If it’s genuinely dull to you, that can wear you down over time.

### 4. How reversible is this choice?
This is huge.

If you take Meta and discover:
- management is not for you, or
- the domain bores you,

how easy is it to return to:
- an IC role,
- research-heavy work,
- or a similar level/company?

If the move is reversible, that increases the value of experimenting.

### 5. What are the hidden variables?
The research says these often matter more than people think:

- who would be your manager?
- what is the quality of the team?
- how political is the environment?
- expected working hours?
- how success is measured?
- how much autonomy will you actually have?
- how much of your time will still be technical?

A very interesting role with a bad local environment can be miserable.  
A less intellectually pure role with excellent people and autonomy can be great.

## My best evidence-based summary

If I compress the research into one practical statement, it would be:

> **Interesting work is genuinely important, but not important enough that you should maximize it at all costs.**

A better target is:

> **a role that is interesting enough, while also giving you autonomy, growth, good relationships, manageable stress, and alignment with who you want to become.**

That “who you want to become” part is especially relevant here because team leadership is something you’ve wanted.

## My personal read of your case

Based only on what you wrote:

- If the Meta work is just **somewhat less interesting**, but the leadership opportunity is real and you’ve wanted it for a long time, I would take that very seriously.
- If the work is **substantially less interesting** and the leadership scope is ambiguous or weak, I’d be more cautious.
- If at Google you can plausibly get a leadership path internally within, say, 6–18 months, that changes the calculation a lot.

The research would not support “always stay with the most interesting work.”  
It would support: **don’t give up strong intrinsic interest lightly, but don’t ignore a major growth/identity opportunity either.**

## A simple decision rule you might use

Try rating each job from 1–10 on:

- intellectual interest
- autonomy
- growth
- leadership development
- manager quality
- team quality
- stress sustainability
- compensation
- reversibility
- long-term identity fit

Then ask:

1. Which role wins on total?
2. Which role would I regret not trying?
3. Which role better fits the person I want to be in 3 years?

Often the third question is the most clarifying.

If you want, I can help you build a **research-informed decision matrix** specifically for your Google vs Meta choice.

answer:
Short answer: **the research mostly does *not* support the idea that “interesting work doesn’t matter much.”** If anything, it tends to show the opposite:

- **Interesting / meaningful / intrinsically motivating work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction**
- **Pay matters, but usually less than people think once compensation is already high**
- **The catch:** higher job satisfaction does **not automatically translate into dramatically higher overall life happiness**, because life satisfaction depends on many other things too

So the research-based answer is more like:

> “Choosing the most interesting job possible” is **not always optimal**, but **choosing a job that is clearly less interesting** usually does come with a real well-being cost unless it gives you something else important back (autonomy, leadership, growth, lifestyle, mission, people, etc.).

## What the literature generally finds

### 1. Job design matters a lot
A classic result in organizational psychology is that people are happier and more motivated when work has things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **sense of ownership / identity**

That comes from the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses broadly support it. In plain English: work tends to feel better when it is engaging, meaningful, and gives you room to operate.

That is pretty close to what people usually mean by “interesting.”

### 2. Intrinsic motivation is usually better for well-being than extra money
A big body of work from **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) finds that well-being is strongly tied to whether work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

A job that is intellectually engaging often scores well on autonomy + competence. A leadership role might score well on autonomy + relatedness + impact, depending on the environment.

This is one reason two jobs with similar prestige can feel very different psychologically.

### 3. Salary has a positive but modest relationship with job satisfaction
Research generally finds that the correlation between **pay** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but not huge**. Once someone is already well compensated, an incremental increase in salary often has less impact than people expect.

That doesn’t mean money is irrelevant. It matters a lot when it changes:

- financial security
- flexibility
- family options
- stress
- location / lifestyle

But for already high-paid workers, the marginal happiness gain from “a bit more” is often smaller than the gain from better work design or better fit.

### 4. Job satisfaction and life satisfaction are related, but not identical
This is important for your question.

Research generally finds a **moderate** link between **job satisfaction** and **overall life satisfaction**. So:

- a more interesting job will often make you **more satisfied with work**
- but it may make you only **somewhat happier overall**, depending on the rest of your life

This is where some people get confused. They hear “interesting work is important,” then look at “overall happiness” and see a smaller effect than expected.

That’s not because interesting work doesn’t matter. It’s because:

- work is only one part of life
- people adapt to some job features over time
- relationships, health, control over time, commute, manager quality, and stress also matter a lot

## So is there evidence for “people are not much happier when they pick the most interesting job possible”?

I would phrase it this way:

### Supported:
- **Maximizing interestingness at all costs is not clearly optimal**
- Once a job is “interesting enough,” other dimensions may matter more:
  - manager
  - autonomy
  - growth
  - hours / stress
  - colleagues
  - mission
  - geographic flexibility
  - chance to develop toward the person you want to become

### Not really supported:
- **“Interesting work barely matters”**
- **“People aren’t much happier with more interesting jobs”**

That broad claim would go against a lot of the literature.

## A subtle point relevant to your case

You’re not choosing between:

- interesting work vs boring work

You’re choosing between:

- **interesting individual-contributor research**
- **less interesting domain/content, but first real leadership opportunity**

Those are psychologically very different.

If you’ve “always wanted to lead a small team,” that leadership opportunity may itself be a major source of intrinsic satisfaction. For some people, managing and building a team is deeply interesting, even if the technical problem is less exciting.

So the right comparison is not just:

- “How interesting is the topic?”

but:

- “What kind of work do I want my days to contain?”
- “Do I want to become a stronger research IC, or start becoming a leader?”
- “Which path will I regret *not* trying?”

## What usually predicts regret better than “interestingness”

In people at your stage, long-run regret often comes more from **identity mismatch** than from slightly lower day-to-day enjoyment.

Examples:

- If you really want to become a leader and keep postponing it, you may regret staying even if the research is cooler.
- If what you truly love is deep technical/research work, you may regret moving into management too early even with more pay and title.

So the key question is less:

> “Will the less interesting job make me less happy?”

and more:

> “Which role is more aligned with who I want to become over the next 3–5 years?”

## A research-consistent decision rule

A good evidence-based way to think about it is to score each option on these dimensions:

1. **Intrinsic interest** — How much do I enjoy the actual work?
2. **Autonomy** — How much control will I have?
3. **Mastery / growth** — Which role grows skills I care about?
4. **Meaning / impact** — Does the work feel worthwhile?
5. **Relationships** — Manager, team quality, culture
6. **Stress / sustainability** — Hours, emotional load, politics
7. **Career capital** — Which opens better future options?
8. **Compensation** — Does the extra money materially change my life?
9. **Reversibility** — How easy is it to come back if I’m wrong?

This often leads to a clearer answer than “interesting vs salary.”

## My best summary

If you’re asking strictly about research:

- **Interesting work does seem to matter a lot for job satisfaction**
- **It matters less for overall happiness than people sometimes imply**
- **But there is not strong support for the view that it barely matters**
- **The more accurate view is that interestingness is one of several major drivers, and its value depends on fit**

For someone in your position, the biggest issue may be:

- not Meta vs Google
- but **IC identity vs leadership identity**

That may dominate the “interestingness” question.

If you want, I can also help you think through this as a concrete decision between the two offers rather than just from the research angle.

answer:
Short answer: the research does **not** generally say “interesting work doesn’t matter much.” If anything, it usually finds the opposite:

- **Interesting / intrinsically motivating / meaningful work is reliably associated with higher job satisfaction and better well-being.**
- But the effect is usually **much stronger for job satisfaction than for overall life happiness**.
- And once both options are already **high-status, well-paid, non-toxic jobs**, the difference in total life happiness may indeed be **smaller than people imagine**.

So the best evidence is more like:

> “Interesting work matters a lot for how good work feels, but it may not dramatically transform your whole life happiness, especially when comparing two already-elite jobs.”

## What research actually tends to measure

“Interesting” is not usually studied as a single variable. Researchers break it into things like:

- **intrinsic motivation**: is the work itself engaging?
- **meaningfulness**: does it feel worthwhile?
- **autonomy**: do you have control?
- **skill variety / challenge**: do you get to learn and use your abilities?
- **person-job fit**: does the role fit your values and strengths?

These are strongly related to how satisfied people are at work.

## Classic research finding: job design matters a lot

A lot of organizational psychology goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham): jobs are better when they have more:

- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

Meta-analytic work since then has generally found that these kinds of job characteristics are **moderately to strongly associated** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower burnout

So if by “interesting” you mean “I enjoy the actual work and feel engaged by it,” that usually **does** matter.

## Meaningful work also tends to help

There are also meta-analyses on **meaningful work** showing it is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- greater sense of life meaning
- somewhat better general well-being

Again, though, the effect is often stronger on **work-related outcomes** than on global happiness.

That distinction is important:
- You might be **much happier at 2pm on a Tuesday** in the more interesting job.
- But only **a bit happier overall** when rating your life 1–10.

## Why the “interesting job” effect may be smaller than people expect

Here are a few reasons the effect can be real but not huge:

### 1. Hedonic adaptation
People adapt to good jobs, just like they adapt to higher salaries. The interesting role becomes normal.

### 2. Work is only one part of life
Sleep, relationships, commute, stress, health, family, location, and your manager often matter as much or more for overall happiness.

### 3. Interesting jobs can come with costs
The most intellectually exciting roles may also bring:
- longer hours
- identity fusion with work
- more competition
- more stress / rejection
- fewer leadership opportunities
- worse work-life balance

So “more interesting” is not automatically “better lived experience.”

### 4. Causality is messy
People who are already more energetic, optimistic, or able tend to sort into more interesting jobs. So some studies may overstate the causal effect.

## What the literature does *not* really support

I don’t think the evidence supports a strong claim like:

> “People are not much happier when they choose the most interesting work possible.”

That’s too strong.

A more accurate research-based statement would be:

> “Interesting work usually improves job satisfaction meaningfully, but may improve overall life happiness only modestly, especially after income/security are already high.”

That sounds much closer to your case.

## In your case, there’s another subtlety

Your choice is **not** simply:

- interesting work vs boring work

It’s more like:

- **interesting research content at Google**
vs
- **leadership opportunity + slightly more money + less interesting content at Meta**

From a research perspective, leadership can itself satisfy important psychological needs:

- autonomy
- impact
- status
- growth
- competence
- future option value

So if “leading a team” is something you’ve genuinely wanted for a long time, then the Meta role may score lower on **subject-matter interest**, but higher on **other intrinsic rewards**.

That means the decision is really about **which kind of interestingness you value more**:
- fascination with the work itself
- or growth into the role you want to become

## A useful way to think about it

Instead of asking:

> “Will the less interesting job make me much less happy?”

ask:

> “Which job has the better daily experience and the better long-term fit for the person I want to become?”

Research suggests that the biggest predictors are often not prestige or abstract salary differences, but things like:

- autonomy
- manager quality
- team quality
- learning
- stress load
- sense of progress
- alignment with values
- whether you use strengths you care about

## My synthesis

If you’re already financially comfortable at Google, then the extra salary alone is unlikely to change happiness much.

The things that are more likely to matter are:

1. **How much you care about the actual subject matter**
2. **How real the leadership opportunity is**
3. **Whether managing people energizes or drains you**
4. **What your day-to-day will actually look like**
5. **Whether this move increases long-term option value**

So the research-based answer is:

- **No, it’s not true that interesting work doesn’t matter.**
- **Yes, it’s plausible that the difference in overall happiness between these two jobs would be smaller than people romantically assume.**
- Especially because both jobs are already likely above the threshold where basic compensation/security are the main drivers.

If you want, I can help you turn this into a **decision framework grounded in the research**—for example a 6-factor scorecard for Google vs Meta.

- **Interesting/meaningful work does reliably predict higher job satisfaction and engagement.**

## What research tends to show

### 1. Intrinsic job quality matters a lot
A large organizational psychology literature finds that people are more satisfied when their work has things like:

- autonomy
- skill variety
- challenge
- task significance
- feedback
- good social relationships

This comes from classic work design research (Hackman & Oldham) and later meta-analyses such as **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**.

In plain English: if the work itself is engaging and lets you grow, that usually matters a lot for job satisfaction.

### 2. “Fit” matters more than abstract prestige
Research on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** finds that people are happier when the role matches what they actually value and enjoy, not just what sounds impressive on paper. A relevant meta-analysis is **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)**.

That’s important for your case, because your choice is not just:

- interesting vs less interesting

It’s also:

- individual-contributor research vs leading people
- deep technical craft vs broader organizational impact
- current enjoyment vs desired future identity

If you’ve “always wanted” to lead a small team, that is itself an intrinsic preference, not just a career trophy.

### 3. Money matters, but usually less than people think at the margin
There is research showing that **pay is positively related to job satisfaction**, but the relationship is typically **modest**, especially compared with day-to-day work experience. A common reference here is **Judge et al. (2010)** on pay and job satisfaction.

For someone already at Google-level compensation, a “bit higher salary” from Meta is unlikely, by itself, to drive a huge happiness difference unless it meaningfully changes your life options or reduces financial stress.

### 4. Meaningful/interesting work often helps satisfaction more than moment-to-moment pleasure
This is a subtle but important point.

Some research suggests that “good jobs” can increase:

- sense of meaning
- pride
- identity
- long-term satisfaction

without necessarily making every day feel more pleasant in the moment, because such jobs can also bring:

- pressure
- stress
- responsibility
- cognitive load

So it’s quite possible for a more interesting role to make you feel **more fulfilled**, but not dramatically more **hedonically happy** hour-to-hour.

That may be why some people overstate the effect of “interesting work” in conversation: they’re mixing together meaning, identity, and happiness.

## So is there evidence that “choosing the most interesting possible job” does not make people much happier?

Yes, in a qualified sense:

1. **There are diminishing returns.**  
   Going from boring, low-autonomy work to engaging work matters a lot.  
   Going from “already very good” to “maximally intellectually exciting” may matter much less.

2. **People adapt.**  
   You get used to salary, prestige, and even interesting projects. The day-to-day structure of the role still matters, but the initial emotional boost fades.

3. **Other factors can swamp interestingness.**  
   Bad manager, bad culture, poor work-life balance, constant firefighting, political overhead, or lack of control can make a fascinating domain miserable.

4. **Interest is not one-dimensional.**  
   You may find the subject matter less interesting, but the leadership challenge more interesting.

So if your question is:

> “Will taking the absolutely most interesting technical work available necessarily make me much happier than a slightly less interesting job with better fit on other dimensions?”

The research-based answer is: **probably not necessarily**.

## What I’d infer for your situation

You’re comparing two elite jobs, not good vs bad jobs. That changes the calculus.

At that level, the likely happiness differences are often driven less by raw salary and more by:

- how much autonomy you’ll have
- whether you like your manager
- whether the team is strong
- whether the day-to-day work energizes or drains you
- whether the role fits the person you want to become

And the biggest hidden issue in your description is this:

> “leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do”

That is not a side note. It may be the most important sentence.

Because if leadership is a real aspiration, then the Meta role is not simply “less interesting.” It may be:

- less interesting in **research topic**
- more interesting in **career development**
- more aligned with **identity**
- higher on **impact through others**
- potentially lower on **daily intellectual enjoyment**

That is a very different tradeoff.

## A practical way to think about it

Instead of asking:

> “Which job is more interesting?”

ask:

> “Which job gives me the better weekly lived experience for the next 2–3 years?”

Rate each option on:

- interest in daily tasks
- autonomy
- manager quality
- team quality
- growth/learning
- leadership development
- stress level
- work-life balance
- mission/product belief
- future option value

Then ask two separate questions:

### A. What will make me happier day-to-day?
Usually:
- interesting work
- good teammates
- autonomy
- sane workload

### B. What will make me more satisfied with my trajectory?
Often:
- doing something aligned with long-term identity
- building skills you genuinely want
- not feeling you chickened out of something important to you

These can point in different directions.

## My honest summary

If you want the research answer, it is:

- **No, the evidence does not support the idea that maximizing “interestingness” is the master key to happiness.**
- But **yes, interesting/meaningful work is a real and important predictor of job satisfaction**, and usually more important than a small salary bump.
- In high-end jobs, **fit** tends to matter more than any single variable.

So I would not frame this as:

- Google = happiness because interesting
- Meta = unhappiness because less interesting

I would frame it as:

- **Google:** probably better immediate intellectual enjoyment
- **Meta:** possibly better alignment with your desire to lead

If you deeply want to try leadership, the research would not tell you to ignore that in favor of pure task-interest. Leadership aspiration is itself part of what makes work meaningful.

## My tentative advice
A small salary increase should get very little weight here.

The real question is:
**Do you want to optimize for fascinating work now, or for becoming the kind of person who leads teams?**

If you’re unsure, the best next step is not abstract reflection but information-gathering:

- talk to 3 people doing that exact Meta role
- ask how much of the week is hiring/perf/process/politics vs actual thinking
- ask whether former research ICs felt fulfilled or frustrated
- ask at Google whether there’s a credible path to team leadership internally

That last point matters a lot: if you can get leadership experience **without** giving up the more interesting work, that may dominate both options.

If you want, I can help you build a decision framework specifically for this Google-vs-Meta tradeoff.

answer:
Short answer: **research does not really support the idea that “interesting work barely matters.”** If anything, it suggests the opposite:

- **Interesting / meaningful / intrinsically motivating work tends to matter a lot for job satisfaction**
- It matters **somewhat less, but still meaningfully, for overall life happiness**
- But **maximizing interestingness at all costs is not obviously optimal**, because other factors matter too: autonomy, growth, manager quality, coworkers, stress, identity fit, and whether the role moves you toward the kind of career you want

So the best-supported conclusion is not:

> “Interesting work doesn’t matter much.”

It’s more like:

> “Interesting work matters a lot, but it is only one important dimension, and people often make mistakes by optimizing a single dimension.”

## What the research generally finds

The exact word “interesting” is not usually what researchers measure. They usually look at nearby concepts:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **person-job fit**
- **autonomy**
- **skill variety / challenge**
- **engagement**

Across those literatures, the pattern is pretty consistent.

### 1. Intrinsic aspects of work are strong predictors of job satisfaction
Classic work-design research and later meta-analyses show that jobs with more:

- autonomy
- variety
- challenge
- feedback
- significance

produce higher job satisfaction and motivation.

This is one of the most replicated findings in organizational psychology.

### 2. Meaningful work is associated with better well-being
There are also meta-analyses showing that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report more:

- job satisfaction
- engagement
- commitment
- life satisfaction

and less burnout / distress.

Not infinite effects, not magic, but definitely not “doesn’t matter.”

### 3. Person-job fit matters a lot
If a role fits your interests, values, and strengths, people tend to do better psychologically. A mismatch often creates that “I’m objectively doing well, but I feel flat” feeling.

### 4. Salary helps, but usually with diminishing returns
Higher pay matters, especially if it changes:

- financial security
- family options
- housing / location
- stress about money

But among already highly paid professionals, the incremental happiness from “somewhat more money” is often **smaller** than people predict, compared with daily experience at work.

So if the Meta salary bump is nice but not life-changing, research would usually say: **don’t overweight it**.

---

## What research does *not* really show

There is **not much evidence for**:

> “Once you have a decent job, whether it’s interesting or not barely affects happiness.”

That would be too strong.

What *is* true is that people often over-romanticize “interesting work” in a vague way. For example:

- novelty can fade
- prestige can be confused with meaning
- intellectually exciting work can come with terrible stress or politics
- “interesting subject matter” is not the same as “good day-to-day life”

So the real question is often not:

> “Which company is more interesting?”

but:

> “Which job will produce a better weekly lived experience and a better long-term fit?”

---

## Your specific case: the key complication

Your choice is not simply:

- **interesting work** vs **more money**

It’s more like:

- **more interesting technical/research content at Google**
vs
- **less interesting content, but a first leadership opportunity at Meta**

That matters because **leadership itself may be intrinsically meaningful to you**.

If “leading a small team” is something you’ve wanted for a long time, then the Meta role may actually be *more* aligned with your deeper motives, even if the technical domain is less exciting.

That’s an important distinction:

- **interesting problems**
- **interesting role identity**

Sometimes people leave a more stimulating domain because they realize what they actually wanted was not “cooler research,” but **ownership, influence, mentorship, and building teams**.

---

## A research-based way to think about it

A useful summary from the literature would be:

### Your happiness is likely to depend more on:
1. **Day-to-day task enjoyment**
2. **Autonomy / control**
3. **Growth toward your desired future self**
4. **Quality of manager and peers**
5. **Stress / hours / politics**
6. **Fit with your values and strengths**

and less on:
- small-to-moderate salary differences
- prestige signaling
- abstract ideas of “this should be the more impressive choice”

---

## A practical test

Try scoring each job from 1–10 on these dimensions:

- Intellectual interest
- Autonomy
- Leadership growth
- Manager quality
- Team quality
- Stress / sustainability
- Mission alignment
- Career capital 2 years from now
- Reversibility if it goes badly
- Compensation after tax, adjusted for actual lifestyle impact

Then weight them by what you actually care about.

That usually produces a better answer than asking “which is more interesting?” in the abstract.

---

## My read, based on the evidence

If you asked me to compress the research into one sentence for your situation, I’d say:

> **Do not assume the more interesting technical work is automatically the better life choice — but also do not assume a modest pay increase and a management title will compensate for being bored every week.**

In other words:

- If the Meta role is merely “less interesting,” but still solid, and gives you a real chance to test leadership — that could be a very good move.
- If it is *substantially* less interesting and you suspect you’ll feel drained or under-stimulated, the salary bump probably won’t make up for it.
- If you can get similar leadership exposure at Google within 6–12 months, staying may dominate.
- If you cannot, the Meta role may be valuable as a career experiment.

---

## The most important question

Not:

> “Which job sounds better?”

But:

> “Which calendar would I rather live for the next two years?”

Because happiness research on work tends to reward the **lived texture of daily work**, not the label.

---

## Bottom line

So to answer your research question directly:

**No, the evidence does not really say that picking the most interesting job possible makes little difference to happiness.**  
It more strongly says:

- interesting / meaningful / intrinsically motivating work **does matter**
- but **not so much that it should dominate every other consideration**
- especially when the alternative offers a different kind of intrinsic value, like leadership, autonomy, or growth

If you want, I can help you think through **your specific Google vs Meta decision** with a structured framework rather than just general research.
